What is RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
Large Language Models are incredibly knowledgeable — until you ask about something that happened yesterday, an internal company document, or a product that never appeared in their training data.
That’s the problem Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) was built to solve.
With RAG, a retrieval system first searches for relevant information and passes it to the LLM, which then generates an answer grounded in that context. Here’s the pipeline these techniques bolt onto:
Why basic RAG breaks

• Dense embeddings prioritize semantic similarity over exact lexical matches. They tend to capture semantic meaning (“the vibe”) rather than exact tokens, and the pure vector search misses these exact matches (error codes, product IDs, acronyms).

• Fixed size chunking slices sentences into pieces. So, the retrieved result is almost-but-not-quite what you need.

• Top-k similarity isn’t the same as top-k relevance — a chunk can score high on the similarity but can be totally useless to answer the actual question.

• There’s no way to say “only search last quarter’s docs” — everything is just one flat pile.
These six techniques below each patch one of these failure points.
1. Hybrid Search (BM25 + Dense)
Imagine two librarians helping you find a book. One has memorized every word in every book — ask for “PostgreSQL vacuum” and she finds every page containing those exact words. The other doesn’t memorize words; she remembers the feeling of every book — ask about “database housekeeping and cleanup” and she’ll find the vacuum page even though you never said “vacuum.” Neither librarian alone is enough. BM25 is librarian one (sparse, keyword, exact-match, great with acronyms/IDs/rare terms). Dense embeddings are librarian two (semantic, handles synonyms and paraphrase, weak on exact tokens).
You run both searches, then merge the two ranked lists — usually with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): a document’s fused score is the sum of 1/(k + rank) across each list it appears in, so something that ranks well in either list bubbles up, without needing to normalize two different scoring scales.
The beauty of RRF is that it only cares about rank, not the underlying scoring scales, so BM25 scores and cosine similarities don’t need to be normalized.
Here’s a great article on Reciprocal Rank Fusion: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) — How to score results from multiple retrieval methods in RAG
Tradeoff: more infra (you need a full-text index alongside the vector index — Postgres FTS + pgvector, or Elasticsearch + a vector store), and RRF’s k constant needs tuning. But it fixes the single biggest naive-RAG failure: queries with specific technical terms.
2. Cross-encoder re-ranking
Hybrid search is fast because it compares your query to each document independently — pre-computed embeddings, quick lookup. But “fast and independent” has a cost: it can’t reason about the query and the document together. A cross-encoder is slower but smarter — it reads the query and candidate side-by-side and gives one focused relevance judgment, like a bouncer who actually checks your ID against the guest list instead of just judging you’re dressed nicely enough.
Concretely: retrieve top ~50 candidates with hybrid search (cheap), then re-score just those 50 with a cross-encoder (expensive per-pair, but only 50 pairs, not the whole corpus), keep the top ~5.
Tradeoff: cross-encoders can’t be pre-computed or indexed — every query needs fresh inference — so you only use them on a small shortlist, never the full corpus. Big precision win at query time, real latency cost per query.
3. Chunk strategy — semantic vs fixed
Fixed-size chunking is cutting a cake with a ruler every 2 inches, ignoring where the actual slices are supposed to be — you might cut straight through a piece of frosting decoration. Semantic chunking cuts along the cake’s natural seams — paragraph breaks, headers, topic shifts — so each piece is a coherent unit.
Fixed (e.g. 512 tokens with overlap) are simple, fast, and predictable but blind to structure — a step-by-step instruction can get split mid-step. Semantic chunking tries to keep related ideas together by splitting on natural topic boundaries, such as headings, paragraphs, or sudden shifts in meaning detected by embeddings, but is slower to compute and can produce wildly uneven chunk sizes, which complicates downstream ranking.
4. Metadata filtering
This is just giving your retrieval system filing-cabinet drawers instead of one giant pile of paper on the floor. “Search only within: department=legal, date > 2025, doc_type = policy” — before or alongside the semantic search.
It’s not a retrieval-quality technique so much as a precision + trust technique: it prevents a stale 2019 doc from outranking the current one just because it happens to phrase things more similarly to the query, and it lets you enforce access control (user can only retrieve docs they’re permitted to see). Cheap to add if your chunks carry metadata; the tradeoff is entirely on the ingestion side — you have to tag documents properly, which is often the unglamorous 80% of the real engineering work.
Metadata filtering also improves latency because irrelevant documents are excluded before ranking.
5. HyDE (hypothetical document embeddings)
Normally you embed the question and search for similar answers — but questions and answers often don’t look alike in embedding space (“How do I fix a leaky faucet?” doesn’t sit close to a paragraph about washer replacement, even though it’s the right answer). HyDE’s trick: ask the LLM to hallucinate (generate a hypothetical answer) a plausible answer first, embed that fake answer instead of the question, and search with it. Fake-answer-shaped text is much closer in embedding space to real-answer-shaped text than a question is.
Tradeoff: an extra LLM call before retrieval even starts (latency + cost), and if the hallucinated answer is confidently wrong in a weird direction, it can drag retrieval off course. Works best for open-ended or under-specified queries; overkill for queries that are already answer-shaped (like a keyword lookup).
6. Parent-child chunking
Think of an index card system in a library — the card is short and searchable, but it points you to the full shelf location where the whole book lives. You search over small, precise child chunks (great for matching), but when one matches, you retrieve the corresponding parent chunk (a full section or page) to give the LLM enough surrounding context to actually answer well.
This solves the classic tension: small chunks retrieve more precisely (less noise diluting the embedding), but small chunks alone often lack the context an LLM needs to generate a good answer. Parent-child decouples “unit for matching” from “unit for generation.”
Tradeoff: more indexing complexity (you store two chunk sizes and a mapping between them) and slightly more tokens sent to the LLM at generation time.

How these stack together
None of these techniques magically make RAG perfect. Instead, each one addresses a different failure mode:

• Hybrid Search fixes lexical misses.

• Re-ranking improves precision.

• Better chunking preserves context.

• Metadata filtering narrows the search space.

• HyDE improves retrieval for ambiguous queries.

• Parent-child chunking balances precision and context.
Production RAG pipelines also tend to apply these techniques from cheapest to most expensive: metadata filtering narrows the search space first, hybrid search casts a wide retrieval net, cross-encoders spend compute only on the shortlisted candidates, and the LLM is reserved for the final answer.
